qa_pairs.question: How can I display a single progress bar for the transfer of all files when using `rsync`?
qa_pairs.answer: To display a single progress bar representing the transfer progress of all files during an `rsync` operation, you can use the `--info=progress2` option. For example: `rsync -azh --no-g --no-p --info=progress2 LOCALNAME someuser@nibi.alliancecan.ca:projects/def-professor/someuser/somedir/`.

qa_pairs.question: How do I use `rsync` to transfer files from a remote system to a local project directory?
qa_pairs.answer: To transfer files from a remote system to a local project directory using `rsync`, you can use a command similar to this: `rsync -avzh --no-g --no-p someuser@nibi.alliancecan.ca:REMOTENAME ~/projects/def-professor/someuser/somedir/`. Here, `REMOTENAME` is the directory or file on the remote system, and `~/projects/def-professor/someuser/somedir/` is the local project directory, which will be created if it doesn't already exist.

qa_pairs.question: What happens if the target directory does not exist when transferring files from a remote system to a local project directory using `rsync`?
qa_pairs.answer: When transferring files from a remote system to a local project directory using `rsync`, if the target directory (e.g., `somedir` in `~/projects/def-professor/someuser/somedir/`) does not already exist, it will be created.

qa_pairs.question: What is an example `rsync` command for transferring files from a local system to a remote project directory?
qa_pairs.answer: An example `rsync` command for transferring files from a local system into a project directory on a remote system is: `rsync -avzh --no-g --no-p LOCALNAME someuser@nibi.alliancecan.ca:projects/def-professor/someuser/somedir/`.
